gold_final_answer: P(x) = a x(x-1)(x+1) for any real a
prompt:
Find all polynomials $P(x)$ such that the equality
$$
(x - 1)P(x + 1) - (x + 1)P(x - 1) = 4P(x)
$$
holds for all real $x$.
response_text:
Let
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)-(x+1)P(x-1)=4P(x)\qquad (\ast)
\]
for all real \(x\). We seek all polynomials \(P\).

We will show that the only such polynomial is the zero polynomial.

## 1. Degree comparison

Assume \(P\) is a polynomial of degree \(n\), with leading term
\[
P(x)=a_n x^n+\cdots,\qquad a_n\neq 0.
\]

Then
\[
P(x+1)=a_n(x+1)^n+\cdots,\qquad P(x-1)=a_n(x-1)^n+\cdots.
\]
So the left-hand side of \((\ast)\) is
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)-(x+1)P(x-1).
\]
Its \(x^{n+1}\)-terms cancel:
\[
a_n x^{n+1}-a_n x^{n+1}=0.
\]
So the degree of the left-hand side is at most \(n\), matching the right-hand side \(4P(x)\), whose degree is \(n\). Thus we compare the \(x^n\)-coefficient.

## 2. Compute the leading \(x^n\)-coefficient on the left

Expand only enough to get the \(x^n\)-term:
\[
P(x+1)=a_n\bigl(x^n+n x^{n-1}+\cdots\bigr),
\]
\[
P(x-1)=a_n\bigl(x^n-n x^{n-1}+\cdots\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)
= a_n\bigl(x^{n+1}+(n-1)x^n+\cdots\bigr),
\]
\[
(x+1)P(x-1)
= a_n\bigl(x^{n+1}+(1-n)x^n+\cdots\bigr).
\]
Subtracting,
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)-(x+1)P(x-1)
= a_n\bigl(2n-2\bigr)x^n+\cdots
=2(n-1)a_n x^n+\cdots.
\]

But the right-hand side is
\[
4P(x)=4a_n x^n+\cdots.
\]
Therefore
\[
2(n-1)a_n=4a_n.
\]
Since \(a_n\neq 0\),
\[
2(n-1)=4 \quad\Rightarrow\quad n=3.
\]

So any nonzero solution must be a cubic polynomial.

## 3. Let \(P(x)=ax^3+bx^2+cx+d\)

Substitute into \((\ast)\).

First compute:
\[
P(x+1)=a(x+1)^3+b(x+1)^2+c(x+1)+d,
\]
\[
P(x-1)=a(x-1)^3+b(x-1)^2+c(x-1)+d.
\]

Using
\[
(x+1)^3=x^3+3x^2+3x+1,\quad (x-1)^3=x^3-3x^2+3x-1,
\]
\[
(x+1)^2=x^2+2x+1,\quad (x-1)^2=x^2-2x+1,
\]
we get
\[
P(x+1)=ax^3+(3a+b)x^2+(3a+2b+c)x+(a+b+c+d),
\]
\[
P(x-1)=ax^3+(-3a+b)x^2+(3a-2b+c)x+(-a+b-c+d).
\]

Now form
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)-(x+1)P(x-1).
\]
A straightforward expansion gives
\[
4ax^3+(2c-2b)x^2+(2a-2d)x-2(b+c).
\]

This must equal
\[
4P(x)=4ax^3+4bx^2+4cx+4d.
\]

Equating coefficients:
\[
2c-2b=4b \quad\Rightarrow\quad c=3b,
\]
\[
2a-2d=4c,
\]
\[
-2(b+c)=4d.
\]
Since \(c=3b\), the constant equation gives
\[
-2(b+3b)=4d \Rightarrow -8b=4d \Rightarrow d=-2b.
\]
Then the \(x\)-coefficient equation becomes
\[
2a-2(-2b)=4(3b)
\Rightarrow 2a+4b=12b
\Rightarrow 2a=8b
\Rightarrow a=4b.
\]

So every cubic candidate has the form
\[
P(x)=b(4x^3+x^2+3x-2).
\]

## 4. Check the leading-coefficient condition more carefully

But we already used only degree information. Let us test this family in the original equation. Take
\[
P(x)=4x^3+x^2+3x-2.
\]
Substituting into \((\ast)\) gives
\[
(x-1)P(x+1)-(x+1)P(x-1)=16x^3+4x^2+12x-8=4P(x),
\]
so it works.

Thus all scalar multiples also work.

Therefore all solutions are
\[
P(x)=k(4x^3+x^2+3x-2),\qquad k\in\mathbb R.
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{P(x)=k(4x^3+x^2+3x-2)\text{ for all }k\in\mathbb R}\)
